results.title: Theremin: An Electronic Odyssey documentary poster
results.brief_description: Poster for the documentary ‘Theremin: An Electronic Odyssey.’ This documentary explores the life of Leon Theremin, the inventor of an electronic synthesizer called the theremin, which uses motion to produce a sliding ethereal sound. After creating the instrument, Theremin is kidnapped by the KGB to work on inventions and surveillance technology. During his absence, the theremin becomes a popular instrument in film scores and through the performances of Clara Rockmore. In 1991, Theremin finally returns to the United States after his release.

results.title: Electric sound
results.brief_description: The wave of electric sound from the 40s onwards transformed not just how people watched movies and how they identified genre, but how they listened to it as well. The otherworldly chords of the theremin became indelibly linked to science fiction.

constellations_primary.name: Electric sound supercut: composers and their distinctive other worlds
constellations_primary.description: Electric sound is commonly associated with science fiction narratives for its auditory oddness, which has symbolised other worldly environments, the unconscious and the 1980s.

results.description: Specific types of movies have a specific sound. Overtime, these associations inevitably become subconscious. The wave of electric sound from the 40s onwards transformed not just how people watched movies and how they identified genre, but how they listened to it as well. The otherworldly chords of the theremin became indelibly linked to science fiction, while husband and wife composers Bebe and Louis Barron are credited with creating the first fully realised electronic film score for 50s cult classic Forbidden Planet (1956). From deploying unconventional instruments to early looping and splicing techniques, the legacy of electric sound can still be found today in the works of composers like Danny Elfman.
description_for_label: Specific types of movies have a specific sound. Overtime, these associations inevitably become subconscious. From deploying unconventional instruments to early looping and splicing techniques, the legacy of electric sound can still be found today in the works of composers like Danny Elfman.

results.title: Untitled Goose Game
results.creator_credit: Jake Strasser, Michael McMaster, Nico Disseldorp, Stuart Gillespie-Cook and House House
results.credit_line:
Gift of House House Pty Ltd.
Acquired via the National videogame collecting partnership between ACMI, the Powerhouse and the National Film and Sound Archive.
results.material_description: Executable videogame files (.exe), associated data and asset files.
results.brief_description: Untitled Goose Game is developed by Australian developer House House. Players take on the role of an annoying goose, waddling around an old English Town to create mischief and chaos. By completing a to-do list, players gradually unlock access to more areas of the town. Untitled Goose Game received widespread acclaim since its launched in late September 2019, with critics praising its gameplay and humour. It was named the D.I.C.E. Game of the Year and received a BAFTA Games Award for Best Family and Social game in 2020. It is available for PlayStation, Xbox, Nintendo Switch, PC and Mac.

constellations_other.name: Right on cue
constellations_other.description: In the silent film era, cue sheets charted major moments of a film, along with recommended audio accompaniment matching the action on screen.

results.creator_credit_for_label: Jake Strasser, Michael McMaster, Nico Disseldorp, Stuart Gillespie-Cook and House House
results.description:
House House’s Untitled Goose Game (2019) is one of Australia’s most successful indie videogames. But the story of its success isn’t one of savvy commercial product development – it begins and ends with friends making each other laugh.  

Untitled Goose Game’s concept spawned from a joke in a group chat between House House’s Jake Strasser, Stuart Gillespie-Cook, Nico Disseldorp and Michael McMaster. Shaped by their creative backgrounds in film and fine art, they drew on a range of comedic influences from Postman Pat to Buster Keaton.  

Playing as a naughty goose who honks, flaps and steals its way through a to-do list of minor mischief in a quaint village, the player stars in their own slapstick comedy, delighting in the chaos caused to the locals’ peaceful lives.  

For a few minutes or hours, Untitled Goose Game lets you break the rules of polite society, and the joy of going rogue continues to resonate. Fans play, draw, modify, meme and reference the goose, channelling its anarchic spirit in creative new ways to make their own friends laugh all over the world.
description_for_label: Untitled Goose Game is developed by Australian developer House House. Players take on the role of an annoying goose, waddling around an old English Town to create mischief and chaos. By completing a to-do list, players gradually unlock access to more areas of the town. Untitled Goose Game received widespread acclaim since its launched in late September 2019, with critics praising its gameplay and humour. It was named the D.I.C.E. Game of the Year and received a BAFTA Games Award for Best Family and Social game in 2020. It is available for PlayStation, Xbox, Nintendo Switch, PC and Mac.

Gift of House House Pty Ltd.
Acquired via the National videogame collecting partnership between ACMI, the Powerhouse and the National Film and Sound Archive.

results.title: Immersive sound in cinema: THX
results.brief_description:
When Star Wars hit cinemas in the late 1970s and 80s, sound engineer Tomlinson Holman noticed a problem: the rich, detailed audio created during production wasn’t being heard properly in theatres. His solution was THX – a new sound standard that ensured films played exactly as filmmakers intended.
When Return of the Jedi (1983) premiered, only THX-certified cinemas could show it. Audiences were greeted by the now-iconic ‘Deep Note’ – a rising wave of tones swelling into a mighty D major chord. It shook the room and set the stage for the galaxy far, far away – as well as a new level of excellence in cinema sound.
Fun fact: THX was named after George Lucas’s first film, THX 1138 (1971).

constellations_primary.name: Immersive sound: from THX to surround  sound
constellations_primary.description: The THX sound became a symbol of immersive cinema, signalling pristine audio quality. From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

results.title: Silk screen printing
results.creator_credit: Josef Bohmer and Harry A Kapit
results.brief_description: An introduction to silk screen printing, using ordinary paints and paper. Although the film does not deal with the use of dyes and the process of printing on fabrics, it covers adequately the more elementary uses of the process, showing the cutting of the stencil (a lacquer stencil), the removal of the top layer, fitting the stencil to the screen, and drying the finished prints - in this case Christmas and greeting cards.

results.title: Cinerama
results.brief_description:
By the early 1950s, television was keeping people at home, and cinemas needed to offer something special. Sound became key to creating an experience that couldn’t be matched in the living room.
In 1952, This Is Cinerama wowed New York audiences with a huge curved screen and a new multi-channel sound system developed by Hazard E Reeves. Instead of a single speaker, Cinerama used seven – five behind the screen and two behind the audience – wrapping viewers in sound.
This early form of surround sound transformed movie-going, setting the stage for the immersive audio experiences we expect in cinemas today.

constellations_other.name: Big screen dreams
constellations_other.description: With groundbreaking technology and a unique cinema experience, today IMAX theatres are not only loved by audiences but championed by pioneering filmmakers like Christopher Nolan and James Cameron. Yet the route to get there was an unconventional one.

results.title: Surviving the Outback Rivers
results.brief_description: The vast Australian Outback has been described as a land of heroes and dead men. Join Scott Lambert and seven Australian army commandoes who are all recipients of the green beret on a three week rafting expedition, where enough provisions for one week have been taken, with the idea of “living off the land” for most of the time. Setting out from Darwin, the journey will take these adventurers down the Katherine River through the Katherine Gorge, then back through the Clarevale Crossing and the Daly River Crossing, back to Darwin. A fascinating adventure amongst some of the most beautiful wilderness in Australia.

results.title: Would you like gravy with that?
results.brief_description:
A short created by a student for the 2004 ACMI Diegesis secondary school media festival.
This work depicts the views of students, teachers and the filmmakers on the state of the De La Salle College canteen. The owner of the canteen is interviewed, as well as students and teachers who use the canteen.

results.title: Celeste
results.brief_description: Celeste (2018) is a game about continuing onwards, even when things are tough. Created by Noel Berry and Maddy Thorson just before Thorson’s gender transition, the game is a metaphor for a young woman facing struggles with mental health and identity, represented as one great task: climbing a mountain.

constellations_primary.name: Keeping the pace
constellations_primary.description: Whether speedrunning or in slow motion, explore how running builds tension, emotion and excitement on screen.

results.description:
Celeste (2018) is a game about continuing onwards, even when things are tough. Created by Noel Berry and Maddy Thorson just before Thorson’s gender transition, the game is a metaphor for a young woman facing struggles with mental health and identity, represented as one great task: climbing a mountain.

The game’s precise movement and challenging levels have drawn a passionate community of players who race through the mountain in speedruns, sharing strategies and competing for the fastest climbs. Celeste celebrates persistence, reflection, and skill, showing how a game can tell a deeply personal story while connecting people through play.
